mn10:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying among the Kurus. Now there is a town of the Kurus called Kammāsadhamma.
mn10:1.3: There the Blessed One addressed the monks,
mn10:2.1: “This is the direct path for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow & lamentation, for the disappearance of pain & distress, for the attainment of the right method, & for the realization of unbinding—in other words, the four establishings of mindfulness.
mn10:3.2: “There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
mn10:3.5: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent,2 alert,3 & mindful4—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.5
mn10:4.1: “And how does a monk remain focused on the body in & of itself?
mn10:4.2: [1] “There is the case where a monk—having gone to the wilderness, to the shade of a tree, or to an empty building—sits down folding his legs crosswise, holding his body erect and establishing mindfulness to the fore.
mn10:4.3: Always mindful, he breathes in; mindful he breathes out.
mn10:4.4: “Breathing in long, he discerns, ‘I am breathing in long’; or breathing out long, he discerns, ‘I am breathing out long.’
mn10:4.5: Or breathing in short, he discerns, ‘I am breathing in short’; or breathing out short, he discerns, ‘I am breathing out short.’
mn10:4.6: He trains himself, ‘I will breathe in sensitive to the entire body’; he trains himself, ‘I will breathe out sensitive to the entire body.’
mn10:4.7: He trains himself, ‘I will breathe in calming bodily fabrication’;8 he trains himself, ‘I will breathe out calming bodily fabrication.’
mn10:4.8: Just as a dexterous turner or his apprentice, when making a long turn, discerns, ‘I am making a long turn,’ or when making a short turn discerns, ‘I am making a short turn’;
mn10:4.9: in the same way the monk, when breathing in long, discerns, ‘I am breathing in long’; or breathing out long, he discerns, ‘I am breathing out long.’ …
mn10:4.11: He trains himself, ‘I will breathe in calming bodily fabrication’; he trains himself, ‘I will breathe out calming bodily fabrication.’
mn10:5.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself.
mn10:5.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body.
mn10:5.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:5.4: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:6.1: [2] “And further, when walking, the monk discerns, ‘I am walking.’ When standing, he discerns, ‘I am standing.’ When sitting, he discerns, ‘I am sitting.’ When lying down, he discerns, ‘I am lying down.’
mn10:6.2: Or however his body is disposed, that is how he discerns it.
mn10:7.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself.
mn10:7.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body.
mn10:7.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:7.4: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:8.1: [3] “And further, when going forward & returning, he makes himself fully alert; when looking toward & looking away… when flexing & extending his limbs… when carrying his outer cloak, his upper robe, & his bowl… when eating, drinking, chewing, & savoring… when urinating & defecating… when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, talking, & remaining silent, he makes himself fully alert.
mn10:9.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself. Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body. Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:9.2: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:10.3: just as if a sack with openings at both ends were full of various kinds of grain—wheat, rice, mung beans, kidney beans, sesame seeds, husked rice—and a man with good eyesight, pouring it out, were to reflect, ‘This is wheat. This is rice. These are mung beans. These are kidney beans. These are sesame seeds. This is husked rice,’
mn10:10.4: in the same way, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things:
mn10:10.5: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, tendons, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, large intestines, small intestines, gorge, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin-oil, saliva, mucus, fluid in the joints, urine.’
mn10:11.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself. Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body. Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:11.2: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:12.3: just as a dexterous butcher or his apprentice, having killed a cow, would sit at a crossroads cutting it up into pieces,
mn10:12.4: the monk reflects on this very body—however it stands, however it is disposed—in terms of properties:
mn10:12.5: ‘In this body there is the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, & the wind property.’9
mn10:13.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself. Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body. Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:13.2: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:14.1: [6] “And further, as if he were to see a corpse cast away in a charnel ground—one day, two days, three days dead—bloated, livid, & festering,
mn10:14.2: he applies it to this very body,
mn10:14.3: ‘This body, too: Such is its nature, such is its future, such its unavoidable fate.’
mn10:15.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself. Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body. Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:15.2: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:16.1: “Or again, as if he were to see a corpse cast away in a charnel ground, being chewed by crows, being chewed by vultures, being chewed by hawks, being chewed by dogs, being chewed by hyenas, being chewed by various other creatures…
mn10:18-23.1: a skeleton smeared with flesh & blood, connected with tendons…
mn10:18-23.2: a fleshless skeleton smeared with blood, connected with tendons…
mn10:18-23.3: a skeleton without flesh or blood, connected with tendons…
mn10:24.1: bones detached from their tendons, scattered in all directions—here a hand bone, there a foot bone, here a shin bone, there a thigh bone, here a hip bone, there a back bone, here a rib, there a chest bone, here a shoulder bone, there a neck bone, here a jaw bone, there a tooth, here a skull…
mn10:26-28.1: the bones whitened, somewhat like the color of shells…
mn10:29.1: the bones piled up, more than a year old…
mn10:30.1: the bones decomposed into a powder:
mn10:30.2: He applies it to this very body, ‘This body, too:
mn10:30.3: Such is its nature, such is its future, such its unavoidable fate.’
mn10:31.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the body in & of itself, or externally on the body in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the body in & of itself.
mn10:31.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the body, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the body, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the body.
mn10:31.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a body’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:31.4: This is how a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself.
mn10:32.1: “And how does a monk remain focused on feelings in & of themselves?
mn10:32.2: There is the case where a monk, when feeling a painful feeling, discerns, ‘I am feeling a painful feeling.’
mn10:32.3: When feeling a pleasant feeling, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a pleasant feeling.’
mn10:32.4: When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling.’
mn10:32.5: “When feeling a painful feeling of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a painful feeling of the flesh.’
mn10:32.6: When feeling a painful feeling not of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a painful feeling not of the flesh.’
mn10:32.7: When feeling a pleasant feeling of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a pleasant feeling of the flesh.’
mn10:32.8: When feeling a pleasant feeling not of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a pleasant feeling not of the flesh.’
mn10:32.9: When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of the flesh.’
mn10:32.10: When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling not of the flesh, he discerns, ‘I am feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling not of the flesh.’
mn10:33.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on feelings in & of themselves, or externally on feelings in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on feelings in & of themselves.
mn10:33.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to feelings, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to feelings, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to feelings.
mn10:33.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are feelings’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:33.4: This is how a monk remains focused on feelings in & of themselves.
mn10:34.1: “And how does a monk remain focused on the mind in & of itself?
mn10:34.2: There is the case where a monk, when the mind has passion, discerns, ‘The mind has passion.’
mn10:34.3: When the mind is without passion, he discerns, ‘The mind is without passion.’
mn10:34.4: When the mind has aversion, he discerns, ‘The mind has aversion.’
mn10:34.5: When the mind is without aversion, he discerns, ‘The mind is without aversion.’
mn10:34.6: When the mind has delusion, he discerns, ‘The mind has delusion.’
mn10:34.7: When the mind is without delusion, he discerns, ‘The mind is without delusion.’
mn10:34.8: “When the mind is constricted, he discerns, ‘The mind is constricted.’
mn10:34.9: When the mind is scattered, he discerns, ‘The mind is scattered.’
mn10:34.10: When the mind is enlarged, he discerns, ‘The mind is enlarged.’
mn10:34.11: When the mind is not enlarged, he discerns, ‘The mind is not enlarged.’
mn10:34.12: When the mind is surpassed, he discerns, ‘The mind is surpassed.’
mn10:34.13: When the mind is unsurpassed, he discerns, ‘The mind is unsurpassed.’
mn10:34.14: When the mind is concentrated, he discerns, ‘The mind is concentrated.’
mn10:34.15: When the mind is not concentrated, he discerns, ‘The mind is not concentrated.’
mn10:34.16: When the mind is released, he discerns, ‘The mind is released.’
mn10:34.17: When the mind is not released, he discerns, ‘The mind is not released.’
mn10:35.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on the mind in & of itself, or externally on the mind in & of itself, or both internally & externally on the mind in & of itself.
mn10:35.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to the mind, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to the mind, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to the mind.
mn10:35.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There is a mind’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:35.4: This is how a monk remains focused on the mind in & of itself.
mn10:36.1: “And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves?
mn10:36.2: [1] “There is the case where a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five hindrances.
mn10:36.3: And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five hindrances?
mn10:36.4: There is the case where, there being sensual desire present within, a monk discerns, ‘There is sensual desire present within me.’ Or, there being no sensual desire present within, he discerns, ‘There is no sensual desire present within me.’ He discerns how there is the arising of unarisen sensual desire. And he discerns how there is the abandoning of sensual desire once it has arisen. And he discerns how there is no further appearance in the future of sensual desire that has been abandoned.
mn10:36.5: [The same formula is repeated for the remaining hindrances: ill will,
mn10:37.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or externally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on mental qualities in & of themselves.
mn10:37.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to mental qualities, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to mental qualities, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to mental qualities.
mn10:37.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are mental qualities’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:37.4: This is how a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five hindrances.
mn10:38.1: [2] “And further, the monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five clinging-aggregates.
mn10:38.2: And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five clinging-aggregates?
mn10:38.3: There is the case where a monk [discerns]:
mn10:38.4: ‘Such is form, such its origination, such its disappearance.
mn10:38.8: Such is consciousness, such its origination, such its disappearance.’16
mn10:39.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or externally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on mental qualities in & of themselves.
mn10:39.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to mental qualities, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to mental qualities, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to mental qualities.
mn10:39.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are mental qualities’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:39.4: This is how a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the five clinging-aggregates.
mn10:40.1: [3] “And further, the monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the sixfold internal & external sense media.
mn10:40.2: And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the sixfold internal & external sense media?
mn10:40.3: There is the case where he discerns the eye, he discerns forms, he discerns the fetter that arises dependent on both. He discerns how there is the arising of an unarisen fetter. And he discerns how there is the abandoning of a fetter once it has arisen. And he discerns how there is no further appearance in the future of a fetter that has been abandoned.
mn10:40.4: [The same formula is repeated for the remaining sense media: ear,
mn10:41.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or externally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on mental qualities in & of themselves.
mn10:41.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to mental qualities, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to mental qualities, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to mental qualities.
mn10:41.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are mental qualities’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:41.4: This is how a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the sixfold internal & external sense media.
mn10:42.1: [4] “And further, the monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the seven factors for awakening.
mn10:42.2: And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the seven factors for awakening?
mn10:42.3: There is the case where, there being mindfulness as a factor for awakening present within, he discerns, ‘Mindfulness as a factor for awakening is present within me.’ Or, there being no mindfulness as a factor for awakening present within, he discerns, ‘Mindfulness as a factor for awakening is not present within me.’ He discerns how there is the arising of unarisen mindfulness as a factor for awakening. And he discerns how there is the culmination of the development of mindfulness as a factor for awakening once it has arisen.
mn10:42.4: [The same formula is repeated for the remaining factors for awakening: analysis of qualities,
mn10:43.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or externally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on mental qualities in & of themselves.
mn10:43.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to mental qualities, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to mental qualities, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to mental qualities.
mn10:43.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are mental qualities’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:43.4: This is how a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the seven factors for awakening.
mn10:44.1: [5] “And further, the monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the four noble truths.
mn10:44.2: And how does a monk remain focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the four noble truths?
mn10:44.3: There is the case where he discerns, as it has come to be, that ‘This is stress…This is the origination of stress…This is the cessation of stress…This is the way leading to the cessation of stress.’
mn10:45.1: “In this way he remains focused internally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or externally on mental qualities in & of themselves, or both internally & externally on mental qualities in & of themselves.
mn10:45.2: Or he remains focused on the phenomenon of origination with regard to mental qualities, on the phenomenon of passing away with regard to mental qualities, or on the phenomenon of origination & passing away with regard to mental qualities.
mn10:45.3: Or his mindfulness that ‘There are mental qualities’ is maintained to the extent of knowledge & remembrance. And he remains independent, unsustained by [not clinging to] anything in the world.
mn10:45.4: This is how a monk remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves with reference to the four noble truths.
mn10:46.1: [E. Conclusion] “Now, if anyone would develop these four establishings of mindfulness in this way for seven years, one of two fruits can be expected for him:
mn10:46.2: either gnosis right here & now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.
mn10:46.4: If anyone would develop these four establishings of mindfulness in this way for six years…
mn10:46.22: If anyone would develop these four establishings of mindfulness in this way for seven days, one of two fruits can be expected for him:
mn10:46.23: either gnosis right here & now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.
mn10:47.1: “‘This is the direct path for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow & lamentation, for the disappearance of pain & distress, for the attainment of the right method, & for the realization of unbinding—in other words, the four establishings of mindfulness.’
mn10:47.2: Thus was it said, and in reference to this was it said.”
mn10:47.3: That is what the Blessed One said.
mn10:47.4: Gratified, the monks delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
